standard.title: Forces and Motion
standard.description: Investigate and represent balanced and unbalanced forces, including gravitational force, acting on objects, and relate changes in an object's motion to its mass and the magnitude and direction of forces acting on it
standard.prior_knowledge: Students can identify pushes and pulls as forces and know that gravity pulls objects toward Earth. They have not represented forces as vectors, analysed balanced/unbalanced states, or applied F=ma reasoning.
standard.progression_endpoint: Students represent and explain how balanced and unbalanced forces, including gravity, relate to changes in an object's motion in terms of mass, magnitude and direction.

nodes.label: Forces as vectors: magnitude and direction
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can represent forces on an object as vectors showing correct magnitude and direction and name the forces
nodes.minimum_width: Represent at least two forces on an object using arrows with correct relative size and direction; name the forces
nodes.typical_width: Draw force diagrams for 4 different scenarios (stationary, accelerating, decelerating, falling); justify arrow choices
wider: Measure the magnitude of forces using a force meter; represent magnitude and direction using force arrow diagrams
Analyse a real photograph of an object in motion — identify all forces acting and represent them in a diagram
Investigate forces acting on boomerangs and how First Peoples of Australia designed an aerofoil profile — represent forces involved
success_criteria: Represents at least 2 forces on an object with correct arrow direction and relative size
Diagram is consistent with the described scenario

nodes.label: Balanced forces and equilibrium
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can identify whether forces on an object are balanced or unbalanced and predict the resulting motion state
nodes.minimum_width: Define balanced forces; correctly identify whether forces are balanced in a given diagram; state that balanced forces mean no change in motion
nodes.typical_width: Analyse 6 scenarios — classify each as balanced or unbalanced; predict the motion state for each with reasoning
wider: Analyse the effect of balanced and unbalanced forces on an object's motion including starting, stopping and changing direction
Investigate a real engineering structure (e.g. bridge, building) — identify how balanced forces are maintained
Investigate how simple machines such as levers and pulleys change the magnitude of force needed — represent forces involved
nodes.hinge_reason: The misconception that balanced forces mean no forces (rather than equal and opposite) is extremely common and directly undermines Newton's First Law. This error propagates into every subsequent node.
success_criteria: Correctly identifies balanced vs unbalanced forces in a given diagram
States that balanced forces mean no change in motion
Predicts the motion state from a force diagram

nodes.label: Unbalanced forces and changes in motion
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can explain that unbalanced forces cause a change in motion and represent this with force diagrams
nodes.minimum_width: Explain that unbalanced forces cause a change in motion (speed or direction); give two examples with force diagrams
nodes.typical_width: Analyse motion scenarios — identify the unbalanced force, predict the change in motion, draw the force diagram
wider: Investigate the effects of applying different forces to familiar objects of the same and different mass
Design a fair test to investigate how the magnitude of an unbalanced force affects the rate of motion change
Investigate forces through application of traditional tools such as spearthrowers used by First Peoples of Australia — analyse the forces involved
success_criteria: States that unbalanced forces cause a change in motion
Gives 2 examples with correct force diagrams
Identifies both speed and direction as possible changes

nodes.label: Gravitational force and mass
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can distinguish mass from weight, explain gravitational force as dependent on mass, and calculate weight from mass
nodes.minimum_width: Distinguish mass from weight; explain gravitational force as dependent on mass; calculate weight given mass and gravitational field strength
nodes.typical_width: Compare gravitational force on the same object on Earth, the Moon, and Mars — explain differences using gravitational field strength
wider: Investigate how Earth's gravitational force pulls objects to the centre of Earth and how its magnitude relates to mass
Examine how gravity affects objects in space including moons, planets, stars and galaxies
Evaluate a common misconception (e.g. 'objects fall at different rates based on mass') using experimental evidence; construct a refutation
nodes.hinge_reason: Mass/weight confusion is the most persistent misconception in this standard and is directly assessed. Students who conflate the two make systematic errors in force calculations and cannot correctly explain gravitational scenarios.
success_criteria: Correctly distinguishes mass from weight with definitions
Calculates weight correctly given mass and g
Explains gravitational force as dependent on mass

nodes.label: Relating force, mass and motion change
nodes.progression_checkpoint: Students can describe the qualitative relationship between force, mass and acceleration and interpret data from force investigations
nodes.minimum_width: State the relationship between force, mass and acceleration qualitatively; predict whether a heavier or lighter object will accelerate more under the same force
nodes.typical_width: Analyse data from a force investigation — identify the pattern between force, mass and acceleration; represent graphically
wider: Evaluate a real-world application (e.g. vehicle safety, rocket launch) — explain how engineers manipulate force and mass
Investigate how simple machines change force magnitude for a task; analyse the trade-offs involved
Analyse the forces acting on a boomerang in flight — explain how design variables affect the motion outcome
success_criteria: States the qualitative relationship between force, mass and acceleration
Correctly predicts which object accelerates more under the same force
Identifies the pattern in data from a force investigation
